titleStmt.title: The Secretary of State to the Minister in Iran ( (Dreyfus))

div.head: The Secretary of State to the Minister in Iran ( Dreyfus )
div.p: 220. Your 260, August 14. You may inform Prime Minister his request is being considered by Department which is consulting War Department.

Department feels that an American military mission would be most helpful in strengthening our position in Iran at the present time and in building a firm foundation for future relations. It would seem that efficient and honest administration of the Iranian Army by Americans would inevitably exert a powerful influence in our favor among the troops and should go a long way toward counteracting existing pro-Axis feeling. It would also place us in a position to observe and control any movement within the army tending towards its use as a fifth column in the event of threatened Axis invasion. We further feel that an American group in key positions in the army would be of great assistance to the various American advisers, in particular to the financial mission.

These considerations, together with the probability that the army could, within a short time, be made a more effective force for the maintenance of internal order, seem to us to provide sufficient reason for sending a mission. It does not seem necessary at present to consider whether or not Iranian Army might ultimately be brought into the war on the Allied side, although this is a possibility which might take on importance if war should last 2 or 3 years longer. We do not consider it necessary or desirable to exact commitments from the Iranian Government at this time, since, as you have reported, such commitments would have no real support in popular sentiment and might be resented by the people. We would prefer first to lay a firm foundation, aiming for the time being merely at keeping the country quiet and putting it on its feet, in the belief that ultimately the people would be brought to see that their real interests lie on the side of the United States. This, as Department understands it, is basic purpose behind our efforts to supply American advisers in all fields.

In view of the British initiative mentioned in Department’s 138, May 27, we are sure British will wholeheartedly favor an American military mission, and we have no reason to suppose that Soviet Government will object.
We are, accordingly, recommending to War Department that favorable action be taken on Iranian request.
